content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
921 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

A-75-156

November 26, 1975


J. Anthony Kline
Legal Affairs Secretary
Governor's Office
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 75-193

Dear Mr. Kline:

    Thank you for your letter of November 11, 1975,
requesting an opinion with regard to attendance at
fund raising dinners by specified members of the
Governor's staff. This letter is to advise you that
no formal opinion will be issued in response to your
request.

    The Executive Secretary to the Governor, the
Legal Affairs Secretary to the Governor, the Governor's
Director of Programs and Policy and the Governor's
Press Secretary do not have any income reporting obliga-
tions under the Political Reform Act other than those
which will be imposed by the Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by the Governor's Office. Government Code Sec-
tions 87300, et seq. Whether a member of the Governor's
staff who attends a political fund raising dinner with-
out purchasing a ticket has received a reportable benefit
and whether attendance for the specified purpose of
making a speech should alter any reporting obligation
imposed, should be addressed in that Conflict of Interest
Code, which need not be adopted until sometime after
April 1, 1976. Until that time, neither you nor any
of the other staff members specified in your letter
has any reporting obligations under the Political Reform
Act.

    Although potential reporting obligations under
Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act should be treated
in a Conflict of Interest Code, your question, to the
extent that it raises an issue under Chapter 2 of the
Act, can be answered in the negative. Chapter 4 imposes
reporting obligations on candidates, elected officials
and committees supporting candidates or elected officials.

J. Anthony Kline
November 26, 1975
Page Two


Since you do not fall into any of these categories, you
would not incur a reporting obligation by reason of at-
tendance at a political fund raising dinner without pur-
chasing a ticket. Moreover, even if your attendance were
in behalf of the Governor and, thus, the free ticket argu-
ably was a contribution to him, there would be no reporting
obligation since under these circumstances the Commission's
analysis in the opinion requested by Kenneth Corr, No. 75-094-C,
would be applicable.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec-
tion 18321, a copy of which is attached hereto.

If you have any questions, please contact Natalie
West, an attorney on the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:jp

State of California
GOVERNOR'S OFFICE
SACRAMENTO 95814

(916) 445-1915

EDMUND G. BROWN JR.
GOVERNOR

November 10, 1975


Kenneth Finney, Director
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ken:

Thanks for your letter of October 30 and enclosed
Opinion #75-094(c).

By this letter I am requesting your formal or informal
opinion on the following related questions:

    If the Executive Secretary to the Governor, the
    Legal Affairs Secretary to the Governor, the
    Governor's Director of Programs and Policy, or
    the Governor's Press Secretary attend a political
    fund-raising dinner either in the company of the
    Governor or separately at the invitation of the
    sponsors and without purchasing a ticket, do said
    persons incur any reporting obligation under the
    Political Reform Act?

    If, in the same situation, such persons are
    invited by the sponsors to attend the dinner
    for the specified purpose of making a speech,
    would the reporting requirements be the same?

I will, of course, be most happy to discuss this matter
with you further if you believe that it would be useful.

Very truly yours,


J. ANTHONY KLINE
Legal Affairs Secretary

cc: Gray Davis

The Executive Secretary to the Governor, the Legal Affairs Secretary to the Governor, the Governor's Director of Programs and Policy and the Governor's Press Secretary do not have any income reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act other than those which will be imposed by the Conflict of Interest Code adopted by the Governor's Office. Government Code Sections 87300, et seq. Whether a member of the Governor's staff who attends a political fund raising dinner without purchasing a ticket has received a reportable benefit and whether attendance for the specified purpose of making a speech should alter any reporting obligation imposed, should be addressed in this Conflict of Interest Code, which need not be adopted until sometime after April 1, 1976. Until that time, neither you nor any of the other staff members specified in your letter has any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. Although potential reporting obligations under Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act should be treated in a Conflict of Interest Code, your question, to the extent that it raises an issue under Chapter 4 of the Act, can be answered in the negative. Chapter 4 imposes reporting obligations on candidates, elected officials and committees supporting candidates or elected officials. Since you do not fall into any of those categories, you would not incur a reporting obligation by reason of attendance at a political fund raising dinner without purchasing a ticket. Moreover, even if your attendance were in behalf of the Governor and, thus, the free ticket arguably was a contribution to him, there would be no reporting obligation since under these circumstances the Commission's analysis in the opinion requested by Kenneth Cory, No. 75-094-C, would be applicable. If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321, a copy of which is attached hereto. If you have any questions, please contact Natalie West, an attorney on the Commission staff. Sincerely, [Signature] Daniel H.
